attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents with a 12-month history of episodes where her fingers turn white and can become painful in response to cold. They sometimes stay this way for half an hour even after returning indoors and warming up. She also reports an 8-month history of joint pains affecting the small joints of her hands, accompanied by morning stiffness and joint swelling. Additionally, she has a history of long-standing gastroesophageal reflux disease and has recently been diagnosed with oesophageal dilatation on a barium swallow.

On physical examination, notable findings include puffy fingers and nonpitting oedema of the hands. There is also mild synovitis of the proximal interphalangeal joints. A malar rash is observed on the face. Blood tests reveal:

  • Hb: 102 g/L (115–165)

  • WCC: 4.2 × 109/L (3.5–11.0)

  • PLT: 192 × 109/L (150–450)

  • Creatinine: 81 μmol/L (45–90)

  • CRP: 27 mg/L (<3)

  • Creatine kinase: 600 IU/L (25–250)

  • Serum ANA: 1:2560 (<1:80)

  • Rheumatoid factor: 68 IU/mL (<20)

  • Anti-dsDNA antibodies: negative

  • Myositis immunoblot: Anti-U1 RNP positive but all other myositis specific/associated antibodies negative
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a diagnosis of mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD), which is a condition characterised by a combination of features from systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), systemic sclerosis (SSc), polymyositis/dermatomyositis (PM/DM), and rheumatoid arthritis (RA) together with the presence of high-titre anti-U1 ribonucleoprotein (U1 RNP) antibodies.

MCTD occurs worldwide and affects individuals of all races, with a peak incidence during adolescence and the 20s. It is notably more common in females than males. In the early stages of the disease, patients who develop MCTD often cannot be easily distinguished from those with other well-defined systemic rheumatic diseases. The overlapping features of SLE, SSc, PM/DM, and RA typically manifest sequentially over several years.

The most common symptoms at disease onset include Raynaud's phenomenon, arthralgias, swollen hands, a sausage-like appearance of the fingers, and muscle weakness. These symptoms occur in approximately 90% of patients and usually develop gradually. Other specific organ involvement in MCTD includes:

  • Joint involvement (60%) — typically a non-erosive arthropathy

  • Pulmonary involvement (75%) — interstitial lung disease and pulmonary hypertension (major cause of death)

  • Cardiac involvement (30%) — pericarditis, abnormal ECG, mitral valve prolapse

  • Kidney involvement — mild kidney disease can occur, although it is an uncommon occurrence

  • Gastrointestinal involvement (80%) — Disordered oesophageal motility

While some specific presentations in this patient might suggest SLE (such as the malar rash, arthritis and Raynaud's phenomenon) or RA (arthritis with morning stiffness), as well as SSc (Raynaud's phenomenon and oesophageal dysmotility), the combination of these features leads to the most likely diagnosis of MCTD. The presenting features of this patient is unlikely to meet the diagnostic criteria of any other options.

It should be noted that the disease entity of MCTD as a unique connective tissue disease is controversial and many longitudinal cohorts describe evolution of so-called MCTD patients into more specific autoimmune connective tissue disease diagnoses. In the literature, some authors therefore prefer to consider MCTD to be a form of undifferentiated connective tissue disease.
learningPoints: Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD) is a condition characterised by a combination of features from SLE, SSc, PM/DM, and RA together with the presence of high-titre anti-U1 RNP antibodies.
The most common finding in MCTD is Raynaud's phenomenon, and its absence raises doubts regarding the diagnosis.
references: Alves MR, Isenberg DA. “Mixed connective tissue disease”: a condition in search of an identity. Clinical and Experimental Medicine. 2020 May;20(2):159-66. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7181542/

Ortega-Hernandez OD, Shoenfeld Y. Mixed connective tissue disease: an overview of clinical manifestations, diagnosis and treatment. Best Practice & Research Clinical Rheumatology. 2012 Feb 1;26(1):61-72. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1521694212000101

Rahmouni S, Maatallah K, Ferjani H, Abid L, Kaffel D, Hamdi W. Mixed connective tissue disease: Not always an obvious diagnosis. Clinical Case Reports. 2020 Oct;8(10):1979-83. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/ccr3.3045
